patient_utterances: [Clinician asked: "good afternoon , beverly . good to see you ."] nice to see you too .
[Clinician asked: "so , i know my nurse told you a little bit about dax . um , now , if you do n't mind , i'd like to tell dax a little bit about you ."] go for it .
[Clinician asked: "great . um , beverly's a 24 year old female patient , and i know you have a history of type 2 diabetes and high blood pressure . correct ?"] yep .

[Clinician asked: "okay . so , you're here today for your yearly exam . have n't seen you in a year . how're you doing ?"] i've been doing well . i began working in a bakery this summer which has been super fun. i have really been enjoying working there, but i find myself, since i am so close to so many delicious treats, snacking a lot during shifts and eating a lot more desserts than i normally would.
[Clinician asked: "okay , so , that brings me to your diabetes . so , how have your blood sugars been ?"] uh , not so good .

[Clinician asked: "okay ."] so , they've been up and down because of my diet , i think , because i've been taking my medication as prescribed . um , so , i have n't missed anything there , but my diet has been pretty bad lately .
[Clinician asked: "pretty bad lately ?"] yeah .
[Clinician asked: "okay . and , that's really the only thing that's different is your diet , pretty much ?"] yeah .
[Clinician asked: "and , that's because of the new job and all that ?"] yeah. it has been really challenging to resist the temptation.
[Clinician asked: "it's hard to ignore those desserts , are n't they ?"] yeah .
[Clinician asked: "no willpower like me ?"] no , no willpower .

[Clinician asked: "okay . all right . and , and then , um , i know you've had high blood pressure , hypertension . how is , how have your blood sugars been ? have you been checking them ?"] so , my blood pressure's been good . um , despite my , um , my diabetes levels being up and down .
[Clinician asked: "okay ."] so , that's been good .
[Clinician asked: "okay , so , your blood pressures have been normal . you've been sleeping well ?"] yep , getting a full eight hours .
[Clinician asked: "no concerns about hurting yourself or anything like that ?"] no .
[Clinician asked: "okay , good . okay , and i know my , um , nurse did a review of systems . other than what we've talked about so far , is there anything else that you needed to add ?"] no .
[Clinician asked: "good . so , pretty much status quo except for those things that we've been working on , right ?"] yep .
[Clinician asked: "okay , good . all right . well , let me do a quick physical exam , okay ?"] okay .
[Clinician asked: "okay , squeeze my finger here . good . can you feel me touch out here ?"] yep .
[Clinician asked: "okay . and , can you feel me touch you down here ?"] yep .

[Clinician asked: "okay . no swelling . push your leg out . good . pull it back . mm-hmm . go . good . okay . so , on my exam , um , essentially , your exam is normal . your , um , heart is regular . you do have that grade 2 , um , systolic ejection fraction back ejection murmur that you , um , that we've heard in the"] okay .

[Clinician asked: "hey , dragon , show me the hemoglobin a1c . okay , so , blood sugars have been elevated . your hemoglobin a1c is elevated as well , so that tells me your blood sugars have been a little bit out of control . you're still taking the metformin , correct ?"] yes , i am .
[Clinician asked: "okay , and you take that twice a day ?"] yep .

[Clinician asked: "okay . um , uh , hey , dragon ? show me the vital signs . okay , good news here is your blood pressure's good . everything , um , there is fine . so , um , so , my assessment is , is , um , your first problem is diabetes . it's a little out of control . we'd like that hemoglobin a1c to be under seve"] mm-hmm .
[Clinician asked: "so , um , uh , i'm not gon na change your medication . i think this is diet related , so i want you to try to cut down on the snacking and additional sweets , okay ?"] yeah .

[Clinician asked: "and , i want you to check your blood sugars in the morning . i want you to call them in in about two weeks so we know what your first , um , fasting blood sugar was in the morning , and then , we'll come back and see you in about two months and see if everything has straightened out . if it has n't "] okay .

[Clinician asked: "your second problem is your hypertension . that seems to be relatively stable . i want you to stay on your current medication . you're on li- lisinopril 10 mg once a day . just stay right on that medication . no changes at this point . seems to be doing well , okay ?"] okay .
[Clinician asked: "good . anything else i can do for you today ?"] no . that's it .
[Clinician asked: "good . come with me , we'll get you checked out ."] thank you .
gold_note:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Annual exam.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Beverly Ortiz is a 24-year-old female patient with a history of type 2 diabetes and high blood pressure. She is here today for her yearly exam.

The patient states she has been doing well. She has recently began working at a bakery this summer.

The patient states her diabetes is uncontrolled. Her blood sugars have fluctuated recently, which she attributes to her change in diet due to work. She is trying to improve her diet by removing additional sweets. She has been taking her medication as prescribed and has not missed any doses.

The patient states her blood pressure has been good despite her blood sugar levels being up and down. She has continued taking metformin twice a day.

She has been sleeping well and is able to sleep a full 8 hours. She denies thoughts of self-harm or suicidal ideations.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

• Neurological/Psychological: Grip strength equal bilaterally. Neurologically intact.
• Neck: Supple without thyromegaly or lymphadenopathy. No carotid bruits appreciable.
Lungs are clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi.
• Cardiovascular: Regular rate. Grade 2/6 systolic ejection fraction murmur. No gallops or rubs. No extra heart sounds. Good pulses.

RESULTS

Hemoglobin A1c is elevated.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Beverly Ortiz is a 24-year-old female patient with a history of type 2 diabetes and high blood pressure. She is here today for her yearly exam.

Diabetes mellitus type 2.
• Medical Reasoning: Her blood glucose levels have been uncontrolled. She is still taking metformin twice a day, but does admit to dietary indiscretion with an increase in her sweets intake.. Her most recent hemoglobin A1c was elevated.
• Medical Treatment: Continue metformin twice a day.
• Patient Education and Counseling: I encouraged her to check her fasting blood glucose levels each morning and call them in about 2 weeks. She will follow up in about 2 months, at which time we will discuss further treatment recommendations.

Hypertension.
• Medical Reasoning: Appears to be relatively stable.
• Medical Treatment: Continue lisinopril 10 mg once daily.

Patient Agreements: The patient understands and agrees with the recommended medical treatment plan.

naive.response_text: Beverly, I understand how challenging it must be to resist the temptation of all those delicious treats at work, especially when you're enjoying your job at the bakery. It's completely normal to struggle in such an environment, and I want you to know that I'm here to support you every step of the way. Small changes can make a big difference, so maybe try keeping some healthy snacks at work or finding alternative ways to cope with cravings. Remember, it's okay to slip up occasionally; what matters is that you keep moving forward. You're doing great, and I'm confident that with time and effort, you'll see positive changes. Let's look forward to your next appointment and celebrating your progress together. You've got this!

strong_prompt.response_text:
Thank you for sharing your concerns with me, Beverly. It’s clear that you’re feeling a bit overwhelmed with managing your diabetes, especially with the tempting treats at work. I completely understand how challenging it can be to resist when you’re surrounded by delicious desserts. It’s important to acknowledge how hard you’re trying, and I respect the effort you’re putting in, even with these obstacles.

Given the elevated hemoglobin A1c, it seems like the recent changes in your diet have impacted your blood sugar levels. It’s common for stress or a change in environment to affect eating habits, and I know how tough it must be to stay on track. We’ll focus on small, manageable changes to help you get back on track. For instance, mindful eating and portion control can make a big difference without feeling deprived. I’ll also review your current medication to ensure it’s the right fit for you.

Moving forward, I’d like you to monitor your fasting blood sugar levels daily and call in the readings every few days. We’ll schedule a follow-up in about two months to reassess your levels and adjust the plan if needed. Before you leave, could you summarize the steps we discussed? This will help ensure you’re clear on the plan and any questions you have are addressed. Let’s work together to support your health and keep you feeling your best.

framework_only.response_text:
Nice to see you too, Beverly. It sounds like this has been a busy and exciting time for you with the new job at the bakery.

I want to make sure I explained this clearly — can you tell me, in your own words, what we're going to do next and why?

Let's plan to see each other again in two months. In the meantime, if you have any concerns about your blood sugar levels or need support with your diet, please call us.

full.response_text:
I can hear how frustrating this must be for you — you said, "no , no willpower ." I understand how hard it can be to manage these health issues, and I want to work with you to figure out the best way forward.

Research shows that general-practice patients with type 2 diabetes are often older and have different characteristics compared to those in clinical trials. This means our approach needs to be tailored to your specific situation.

The VA/DoD guideline provides clear recommendations for managing high blood pressure in primary care, which we can apply to your situation.

We'll review the VA/DoD guidelines for high blood pressure management to tailor a plan specifically for you.

We'll discuss how to address the challenges you're facing with managing these conditions.

We'll schedule a follow-up to monitor your progress and adjust the plan as needed.

I want to make sure I explained this clearly — can you tell me, in your own words, what we're going to do next and why?

Let's plan to see each other again in a few weeks. In the meantime, if you have any questions or concerns, please don't hesitate to reach out.
